6.1 Secondary Structure: Regular Ways to Fold the Polypeptide Chain

Pauling’s Rules for Secondary Structure
In the early 1950s, Pauling and collaborators postulated several principles of secondary protein structures that any such structure must obey:
Bond angles and lengths should be like those for respective free amino acids
No atoms should approach one another more closely than allowed by their van der Waals radii
The amide group must remain planar
Noncovalent bonds (particularly hydrogen bonds) stabilize folding processes and products

Three-Dimensional Folding of the Protein Myoglobin
Figure 6.1 Three-dimensional folding of the protein myoglobin.
The representation shows that myoglobin consists exclusively of α-helical secondary structure

Rotation around Bonds in a Polypeptide Backbone (1 of 2)
Figure 6.3 Rotation around the bonds in a polypeptide backbone.
Because the bond between the carbonyl carbon and the nitrogen has a partial double bond character, the atom positions in the amide plane are basically fixed

Rotation around Bonds in a Polypeptide Backbone (2 of 2)
Free rotation is only allowed
about the carbons.
However, this rotation is restricted by steric interactions. Such rotation is described by two angles
termed
(between N and

(between
and the carbonyl C).
Figure 6.3 Rotation around the bonds in a polypeptide backbone.

Four Levels of Protein Structure        (1 of 3)
Primary (1°) structure refers to the amino acid sequence that makes up the protein
Secondary (2°) structure refers to local areas of repeating main chain structure
Tertiary (3°) structure refers to the spatial arrangement of the secondary structural elements in the polypeptide chain
Quaternary (4°) structure refers to the spatial arrangement of multiple polypeptide chains to form multisubunit complexes

Four Levels of Protein Structure        (2 of 3)
Figure 6.2 The four levels of structural organization in proteins.

Four Levels of Protein Structure        (3 of 3)
Figure 6.2 The four levels of structural organization in proteins.

Common Secondary Structure Elements (1 of 7)
α-helix: side chains radiate out from helix axis; H bonds are nearly parallel to the helix axis; often with distinct hydrophilic and hydrophobic faces
β-sheet: stabilized by interchain H bonds; side chains alternate sides of the sheet; strands in β sheets can be either parallel (both strands N→C) or antiparallel (one strand N→C, the other strand C→N)
The
helix meets the Pauling criteria but is not
frequently found in proteins

Common Secondary Structure Elements (2 of 7)
Figure 6.4 The right-handed α helix, β sheet, and
helix.
α-helix
β-sheet
helix

Common Secondary Structure Elements (3 of 7)
Parallel and antiparallel arrangement of β-sheets
Figure 6.5 B Sheets.

Common Secondary Structure Elements (4 of 7)
Side chain positions in an α-helix
Figure 6.7 The positions of side chains in the α helix and β sheet.
In an α-helix, the side chains radiate away from the helical axis. Its center consists backbone atoms, closely packed. The hydrogen bonds that stabilize the helix are shown as yellow dashes.

Common Secondary Structure Elements (5 of 7)
Side chain positions in a β-sheet
Figure 6.7 The positions of side chains in the α helix and β sheet.
In a β-sheet, neighboring side chains are located on opposite faces of the sheet, which is stabilized by main-chain hydrogen bonds between adjacent β-strands.

Common Secondary Structure Elements (7 of 7)
Steric Interactions determine peptide conformation
Figure 6.8 Steric interactions determine peptide conformation.
Certain
and
angles result in
steric clashes, where atoms are closer than their van der Waals radii. These angles and related conformations are not allowed.
The backbone of an α helix results in closely packed atoms that do not sterically clash.

6.2 Fibrous Proteins: Structural Materials of Cells and Tissues

Fibrous Proteins as Structural Materials (1 of 4)
Fibrous proteins are elongated molecules with well-defined secondary structures
Examples include:
keratin—hair, fingernails, feathers, scales, or intermediate filaments (intracellular)
fibroin—silk cocoons
collagen—abundant connective tissue protein; matrix material in bone on which mineral components precipitate

Fibrous Proteins as Structural Materials (2 of 4)
High abundance of certain amino acids in fibrous proteins
Table 6.3 Amino acid composition of some fibrous proteins

Fibrous Proteins as Structural Materials (3 of 4)
High abundance of certain amino acids in fibrous proteins
Table 6.3 Amino acid composition of some fibrous proteins

Fibrous Proteins as Structural Materials (4 of 4)
Note: The three most abundant amino acids in each protein are indicate in Pearson blue. Values are given in mole percent.
39% of this is hydroxyproline.
14% of this is hydroxylysine.
From journal of chemical Information and Modeling (2010) 50:690-700, J. M. Otaki,
M. Tsutsumi, T. Gotoh, and H. Yamamoto, Secondary structure characterization based on amino acid composition and availability in proteins.

α-Keratin
Figure 6.12 The coiled-coil structure of α-keratin intermediate filaments.
In α-keratin, large hydrophobic residues repeat every four positions. The α-helix has 3.6 residues per turn, giving each helix a hydrophobic side, which defines the interface between two long helices in the coiled-coil structure typical of keratin.

Silk Fibroin
Figure 6.13 Theoretical model for the structure of silk fibroin.
The extensive close-packed β sheet of fibroin is interrupted by compact folded regions which provide some elasticity.

Collagen (1 of 3)
It is an abundant connective tissue protein; matrix material in bone, on which mineral components precipitate; triple-strand left-handed helix
It contains hydroxyproline (Hyp) and hydroxylysine

Collagen (2 of 3)
G-X-Y tripeptide motif, where X is Pro and Y is Pro or Hyp, lends itself to triple-strand structure
Polypeptide chains are crosslinked and glycosylated
Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) is a cofactor required for proline hydroxylation; vitamin C deficiency (scurvy) leads to collagen degeneration

Collagen (3 of 3)
Figure 6.14 The structure of collagen fibers.
6.3 Globular Proteins : Tertiary Structure and Functional Diversity

Representations of 3D Structures (1 of 2)
Figure 6.15 The structure of human ubiquitin.

Representations of 3D Structures (2 of 2)
Figure 6.15 The structure of human ubiquitin.

Folding into Defined Structures with Diverse Functions (1 of 2)
Proteins have diverse structures, with varying amounts of helix, sheet, and loop regions
Larger proteins often contain two or more distinct “domains” of compact folded structure
A typical protein “domain” is ~200 amino acids and will fold independently
A domain frequently possesses some defined function (e.g., D N A recognition, oligomerization, cofactor binding, etc.)

Folding into Defined Structures with Diverse Functions (2 of 2)
Figure 6.16 Examples of diversity in protein tertiary structures.

Secondary Structure Motifs, Domains, and Supersecondary Structures
Figure 6.17 Two examples of repeated supersecondary structures within a protein domain.

Classification of Protein Structure (1 of 3)
Protein tertiary structure is characterized by the content of helix and sheet secondary structures, and by defined turns that link these secondary structures
Some proteins are predominantly helix or sheet, others possess a mixture of helix and sheet, or very little defined secondary structure
Not all parts of a globular protein structure can be categorized as helix, sheet, or turn. Such regions are often called “random coil” or, more properly, “irregularly structured regions.”

Classification of Protein Structure (2 of 3)
Domain classes
Figure 6.18 Structural diversity in globular protein domains.

Classification of Protein Structure (3 of 3)
Architecture, topology, and homologous superfamily
Figure 6.18 Structural diversity in globular protein domains.

Common Features of Folded Globular Proteins (1 of 2)
Globular proteins have a nonpolar (hydrophobic) interior and a more hydrophilic exterior
are usually twisted or wrapped into barrel structures
The polypeptide chain can turn corners, for example,
Figure 6.20 Examples of
Figure 6.21 A

Common Features of Folded Globular Proteins (2 of 2)
Distribution of hydrophobic and hydrophilic residues in myoglobin
Figure 6.19 The distribution of hydrophilic and hydrophobic residues in globular proteins.
Hydrophobic residues are green, hydrophilic ones are magenta, and ambivalent ones are black
6.4 Factors Determining Secondary and Tertiary Structure

Information for Protein Folding (1 of 2)
Figure 6.22 The denaturation and refolding of ribonuclease A.
Most of the information specifying the tertiary structure is carried in the amino acid sequence, as shown by Anfinsen’s experiment (carried out in the 1950s and 1960s) with bovine ribonuclease A

Information for Protein Folding (2 of 2)
Thermal denaturation of R N Ase A
Denaturation was measured by
viscosity (☐)
optical rotation (O)
U V absorbance (Δ)
Figure 6.22 The denaturation and refolding of ribonuclease A.

Thermal Denaturation of R N Ase A
The “thermodynamic hypothesis” of protein folding (favorable process under physiological conditions) is supported by the reversibility of folding shown by renaturation followed by repeat denaturation (closed triangles)
Figure 6.22 The denaturation and refolding of ribonuclease A.

Thermodynamics, Folding and Stability of Proteins (1 of 7)
Favorable intramolecular enthalpic interactions
charge-charge interactions (e.g., ionic bonds)
intermolecular hydrogen bonds
van der Waals interactions; proteins are densely packed
Unfavorable loss of conformational entropy
Unfolded state has many conformations (high entropy); folded state has only a few closely related conformations (low entropy)

Thermodynamics, Folding and Stability of Proteins (2 of 7)
Favorable gain of solvent entropy from burying hydrophobic groups (the “hydrophobic effect”)
As hydrophobic side chains cluster in the interior they release ordered solvent molecules from cage-like clathrate structures

Thermodynamics, Folding and Stability of Proteins (3 of 7)
Thermodynamic parameters for folding selected globular proteins
Table 6.4 Thermodynamic parameters for folding of some globular proteins at
aqueous solution

Each dataset has been taken at the pH value where the protein is maximally stable; all are near physiological pH. Data are for the
folding reaction: Denatured (unfolded)
native (folded).

Thermodynamics, Folding and Stability of Proteins (4 of 7)
Details of hydrogen bonding in a typical protein
Figure 6.23 Details of hydrogen bonding in a typical protein.

Thermodynamics, Folding and Stability of Proteins (5 of 7)
Disulfide bonds greatly increase protein stability
Figure 6.26 Thermal denaturation of B P T I.
Bovine trypsin inhibitor has 3 disulfide –S-S– bonds
Effect of –S-S– formation is partly entropic; disulfides reduce the number of conformations possible in the unfolded state

Thermodynamics, Folding and Stability of Proteins (6 of 7)
Disulfide bonds greatly increase protein stability
Figure 6.25 Disulfide bonds in bovine pancreatic trypsin inhibitor (B P T I).

Thermodynamics, Folding and Stability of Proteins (7 of 7)
Binding of ions or prosthetic groups also increase protein stability
Figure 6.27 Ion or prosthetic group binding increases protein stability.
6.5 Dynamics of Globular Protein Structure

Kinetics of Protein Folding (1 of 2)
Levinthal’s paradox (postulated in 1968): If protein samples all possible conformations, rejecting each until it reaches the most stable conformation, folding a protein
could take
years!
But a small protein like R N a s e A (124 residues) folds in about a minute
Not all but only some intermediate states are encountered, including the “molten globule state,” which is a compact partially folded state with native-like secondary structure and folding topology

Kinetics of Protein Folding (2 of 2)
Simplified folding pathway for a protein
Figure 6.28 A simplified representation of the folding pathway for a protein.

Protein Folding Energy Landscapes (1 of 2)
Figure 6.29 Protein folding energy landscapes
Idealized funnel
Rugged landscape

Protein Folding Energy Landscapes (2 of 2)
Figure 6.29 Protein folding energy landscapes
Two-state folding landscape
Landscape with metastable intermediates

Intermediate and Off-Pathway States in Protein Folding
Isomerization of prolyl bonds is catalyzed by peptide prolyl isomerases; the trans isomer is favored by about 1000-fold versus the cis isomer
The formation of non-native disulfide bonds is corrected by protein disulfide isomerases, which catalyze the reduction and reoxidation of –S-S– bonds

Models of Protein Folding and Aggregation (1 of 3)
Figure 6.30 Models of protein folding and aggregation.

Models of Protein Folding and Aggregation (2 of 3)
Figure 6.30 Models of protein folding and aggregation.

Models of Protein Folding and Aggregation (3 of 3)
Role of chaperones
Intracellular chaperone concentrations are very high
Chaperones help prevent intermolecular interactions that lead to the aggregation of proteins
Protection is particularly needed when cell is physiologically stressed (heat, etc.) and proteins begin to unfold

Role of Chaperones (1 of 2)
Structure of Escherichia coli G r o E L/G r o E S chaperonin
Figure 6.31 The GroE L-GroE S chaperonin.

Role of Chaperones (2 of 2)
Schematic of chaperonin function
Figure 6.31 The GroE L-GroE S chaperonin.
Interior is lined with hydrophobic groups early in the process
After A T P and G r o E S bind, a conformational change occurs and the interior presents hydrophilic groups—promoting the proper folding of the protein

Models of Protein Folding and Aggregation
Amyloid fibrils and related diseases
Highly ordered amyloids form from non-native folding intermediates or disordered aggregate states
Prions: Infectious agents that cause disease by inducing amyloid formation on contact
Table 6.5 Examples of amyloid-related human diseases

Amyloid Fibrils and Related Diseases (1 of 2)
Cross β-structures
Figure 6.32 Amyloid fibril structure.

Amyloid Fibrils and Related Diseases (2 of 2)
Example insulin
Figure 6.32 Amyloid fibril structure.
Human insulin in its native conformation
Insulin amyloids
6.6 Prediction of Protein Secondary and Tertiary Structure

Predicting Secondary Structures from Amino Acid Sequences
Empirical methods are about 80% accurate
Based on observed distributions of amino acids in helix vs. sheet conformations (e.g., Ala prefers helix, Val prefers sheet, etc.)
Amphiphilic α-helix shows repeating patterns of side chain polarity every 3–4 residues
Amphiphilic β strand shows repeating patterns of side chain polarity every other residue

Predicting Tertiary Structures from Amino Acid Sequences (1 of 2)
Critical need for accurate prediction from sequence, since structures are known for only about 1% of all known sequences
Prediction of tertiary structure is difficult due to the need to correctly predict interactions between residues that are far apart in the primary structure
Current computational methods are about 60% accurate

Predicting Tertiary Structures from Amino Acid Sequences (2 of 2)
Figure 6.33 Comparison of de novo predictions to X-ray crystal structures.

Quaternary Structures—Symmetries (2 of 5)
Structures with helical symmetry
Figure 6.35 Two helical proteins.
Two structures of helical symmetry, actin and the tobacco mosaic virus
These structures are capable of indefinite growth in length

Quaternary Structures—Symmetries (3 of 5)
Structure with
symmetry
In the transthyretin dimer, the two monomers combine to form a β-sandwich. The dimer has
twofold symmetry about the
axis perpendicular to the
image
Figure 6.36 Examples of common point-group symmetries in multisubunit proteins.

Quaternary Structures—Symmetries (4 of 5)
Structure with
symmetry
Three views of the tetrameric enzyme phosphofructokinase. This enzyme has three 2-fold
axes and thus displays a
symmetry
Figure 6.36 Examples of common point-group symmetries in multisubunit proteins.

Quaternary Structures—Symmetries (5 of 5)
Interaction between trypsin and the bovine pancreatic trypsin inhibitor
This is an example of a heterotypic protein–protein interactions (between entirely different proteins)
Complementary surfaces determine specific interactions
Figure 6.37 Interaction of B P T I with trypsin.
6.7 Tools of Biochemistry

Determining Molecular Masses of Native Protein Structures
Molecular masses of native protein structures can be determined using various techniques such as size exclusion chromatography (comparing protein structures to reference proteins eluting through a resin-filled column), analytical ultracentrifugation (examining the sedimentation equilibrium of a protein structures), or mass spectrometry (identifying to mass-to-charge ratios of all components of a protein structure)

Determining the Number and Approximate Molecular Masses of Protein Structure Subunits (1 of 3)
The number and approximate molecular masses of protein structure subunits can be determined using polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis (P A G E) under denaturing conditions (e.g., high heat) in the presence of sodium dodecylsulfate (S D S) and a reducing agent
Subunit molecular masses are estimated by comparison to reference proteins simultaneously separated in the gel

Determining the Number and Approximate Molecular Masses of Protein Structure Subunits (2 of 3)
Figure 6B.2 S D S-P A G E analysis of an antibody.

Determining the Number and Approximate Molecular Masses of Protein Structure Subunits (3 of 3)
Figure 6B.1 Estimation of protein molecular weight by S D S gel electrophoresis.
Figure 6B.2 S D S-P A G E analysis of an antibody.

Chapter 6 Summary (1 of 3)
All proteins have at least three levels of organization
Genes dictate the primary level, the amino acid sequences, which then dictates the other levels (secondary, tertiary and, in some cases, quaternary structures)
The peptide bond limits the secondary structures that can be formed
Ramachandran plots visualize clusters of these
sterically allowed secondary structures (α-helix, β-
sheet,
helix, β-turn, γ-turn, etc.)

Chapter 6 Summary (2 of 3)
Proteins can be broadly distinguished into fibrous and globular
Fibrous proteins are often elongated, performing structural roles in cells, while globular proteins have more complex tertiary structures and fold into compact shapes
The folding of globular proteins occurs rapidly and spontaneously under standard biochemical conditions
Misfolding is prevented or corrected by chaperones
Many proteins form functional multisubunit assemblies (quaternary structures), for example exhibiting helical symmetry or point-group symmetry

Chapter 6 Summary (3 of 3)
Various biochemical tools and techniques can be used to monitor protein folding processes into certain structures or changes in the conformation of specific structures. Examples are U V/Vis spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, circular dichroism, and nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy
Molecular masses of native protein structures can be studied, for example, by size exclusion chromatography, analytical ultracentrifugation, and mass spectrometry, while the number and approximate molar masses of protein structure subunits can be determined, for example, by denaturing polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis
